cards.back: A function that is allowed to be called in a constant expression. It's declared using the constexpr keyword before the return type.

cards.text: To make a function a constexpr function, use {{c1::constexpr}} keyword {{c2::before the return type}}.

cards.question: Why does this code fail to compile?
cards.answer: calcCircumference() is not a constexpr function, so it cannot be called in a constant expression (the initializer for constexpr variable circumference).
cards.explanation: Normal function calls are not allowed in constant expressions. To fix this, add constexpr before the return type of calcCircumference().

cards.front: When is a constexpr function guaranteed to be evaluated at compile-time?
cards.back: When it's used in a context that requires a constant expression (such as initializing a constexpr variable) AND all its arguments are constant expressions.

cards.front: Can a constexpr function be evaluated at runtime?
cards.back: Yes. When a constexpr function is called with non-constant expression arguments, it evaluates at runtime and returns a non-constexpr result.

cards.concept: Why constexpr functions can evaluate at both compile-time and runtime
cards.explanation: Allowing constexpr functions to evaluate at both times means you can use a single function for both cases. Otherwise, you'd need separate functions with different names for compile-time and runtime, leading to code duplication.
examples: constexpr int max(int x, int y) { return x > y ? x : y; }
int b = max(x, y);            // runtime if x,y not constexpr

cards.text: When a function call is evaluated at compile-time, the compiler {{c1::calculates the return value}} and then {{c2::replaces the function call with the return value}}.

cards.front: What two requirements must be met for a constexpr function to evaluate at compile-time?
cards.back:
• The call to the constexpr function must have arguments that are known at compile-time (constant expressions)
  • All statements and expressions within the constexpr function must be evaluatable at compile-time

cards.question: Will this constexpr function call be evaluated at compile-time or runtime?
cards.explanation: Even though greater() is a constexpr function, the arguments x and y are not constant expressions, so the function call must be evaluated at runtime.

cards.front: What happens when a constexpr function is evaluated at runtime?
cards.back: It evaluates just like a normal (non-constexpr) function would. The constexpr keyword has no effect in this case.

cards.text: The constexpr keyword signals to the {{c1::compiler and other developers}} that a function can be {{c2::used in a constant expression}}.
Normal function calls are {{c1::not allowed}} in {{c2::constant expressions}}, which is why we need {{c3::constexpr functions}}.
A constexpr function evaluates at {{c1::compile-time}} when used in a required constant expression, or at {{c2::runtime}} when called with non-constexpr arguments.
